title: Chapter 1 Quiz: The History of IT Ethics
description: Ten questions on the specific people, dates, inventions, and ideas covered in Chapter 1.

questions.question: Around 3200 BCE, the world's first writing system — cuneiform — was developed in the city-states of which region?
questions.explanation: Cuneiform originated in ancient Mesopotamia — the region we now call Iraq — where scribes pressed reed styluses into wet clay tablets. Its earliest uses were mundane: grain allocations, cattle inventories, and commercial debts. Writing began as an accounting tool before evolving into law, literature, and religion.

questions.question: In Plato's Phaedrus, the Egyptian god who invents writing and presents it to the king for royal approval is named:
questions.explanation: In the myth Socrates tells in the Phaedrus, the Egyptian god Theuth invents writing and presents it to Thamus, the king of Egypt. Theuth claims writing will improve memory and make Egyptians wiser. Thamus rejects the gift, arguing that writing will produce forgetfulness and the mere appearance of wisdom — 'an elixir not of memory, but of reminding.'

questions.question: The Code of Hammurabi (c. 1754 BCE) contained 282 laws and was inscribed on:
options: A bronze tablet hung in the palace throne room
Papyrus scrolls copied and distributed to provincial courts
An eight-foot basalt stele displayed publicly in Babylon
Clay tablets sealed in temple vaults for priestly use
questions.explanation: Hammurabi's Code was inscribed on an eight-foot basalt stele and publicly displayed so that anyone who could read could check whether a judge's ruling conformed to written law. The chapter uses this as an example of writing's double-edged nature: the same technology that constrained arbitrary royal power also enabled the bureaucratic record-keeping that sustained a vast empire.

questions.question: Martin Luther posted his 95 Theses on the door of a church in Wittenberg, Germany, on October 31, 1517. How quickly did printed copies reach most of Europe?
options: Within two days — Luther had arranged printing in advance
Within two months — about the speed of horseback courier routes
Within a year — the Church initially suppressed distribution
Within a decade — literacy was still too limited for faster spread
questions.explanation: Within two weeks of October 31, 1517, friends had the Theses printed in Leipzig, Nuremberg, and Basel. Within two months they had circulated to virtually every corner of the German-speaking world and into France, England, and Italy. The Pope, receiving reports about an obscure German monk's complaint, initially dismissed it as a 'monks' quarrel' — by the time the Church acted, the ideas had already taken root across a continent.

questions.question: In 1450, there were approximately 30,000 books in all of Europe. By 1500 — fifty years after Gutenberg's press began operating — how many were there?
questions.explanation: By 1500, there were approximately ten million books in Europe — a 330-fold increase in fifty years. The cost of a book had fallen by ninety percent. This is one of the starkest statistics in the chapter: it illustrates how the printing press did not just make books cheaper but fundamentally transformed who could access, produce, and challenge written knowledge.

questions.question: On May 24, 1844, Samuel Morse sent the first long-distance telegraph message forty miles from Washington, D.C., to Baltimore. What were the words of that message?
options: "Mr. Watson, come here — I want to see you."
"One small step for man, one giant leap for mankind."
questions.explanation: The message 'What hath God wrought!' — a line from the Book of Numbers chosen by a young woman named Annie Ellsworth — was the first long-distance telegraph transmission. The phrase captured the era's astonishment at a technology that moved information at the speed of electricity. Before the telegraph, the fastest communication moved at the speed of a horse.

questions.question: John Stuart Mill's 1859 book On Liberty introduced the 'harm principle' and coined the phrase 'marketplace of ideas.' Mill argued that even false opinions must be allowed expression because:
options: Suppressing any opinion violates a natural right to self-expression
Governments are incapable of reliably identifying which opinions are false
The collision of ideas — including false ones — is how truth advances
False opinions tend to collapse on their own when exposed to public scrutiny
questions.explanation: Mill argued that if a false opinion is suppressed, we lose the opportunity to understand why it is wrong — and an unexamined belief is a fragile one. If a true opinion is suppressed, we lose the truth. In both cases, open debate produces better epistemic outcomes than censorship. Marx countered that this 'marketplace' is rigged: whoever owns the means of communication controls which voices are amplified.

questions.question: George Orwell — author of Animal Farm (1945) and Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949) — was born with what name?
questions.explanation: George Orwell was born Eric Arthur Blair in British India in 1903. He took the pen name 'George Orwell' as an adult. His novels, written under that name, introduced coinages — 'doublethink,' 'memory hole,' 'Big Brother,' 'Newspeak,' 'thoughtcrime' — that have entered the English language as permanent vocabulary for describing political manipulation and propaganda.

questions.question: The Nazi Volksempfänger ('People's Receiver') was a cheap, standardized radio set rolled out after 1933. By 1939, Germany had the highest per-capita radio ownership in the world. Approximately what share of the German population could be reached by Nazi broadcasts?
questions.explanation: By 1939, Germany had over sixteen million radio sets reaching approximately seventy percent of the population. Nazi Propaganda Minister Joseph Goebbels recognized radio's potential immediately and used it to broadcast Hitler's speeches live to the entire nation. The chapter notes that the Volksempfänger were designed to receive but not transmit — the audience could only listen, not respond — making it structurally suited to one-way propaganda.

questions.question: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989–1991 while working at which institution?
options: MIT's Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory in Cambridge
Bell Labs in Murray Hill, New Jersey
ARPA (the Advanced Research Projects Agency) in Washington, D.C.
CERN, the European particle physics laboratory in Switzerland
questions.explanation: Berners-Lee was a British physicist at CERN in Switzerland when he wrote a 1989 memo proposing a system for managing information across the lab's network. His supervisor returned it with the note 'Vague but exciting.' Two years later he had built the Web. Crucially, he deliberately refused to patent his invention, releasing it as an open standard — a choice the chapter treats as one of the most consequential acts of technological generosity in history.

questions.question: Match each key thinker to the work, invention, or concept most closely associated with them in this chapter.

pairs.right: Phaedrus — critique of writing as mere 'reminding'
The 'banality of evil' and Origins of Totalitarianism
The World Wide Web — released as an open standard

questions.explanation: Each revolution in the chapter has a key thinker: Plato (writing), Gutenberg (printing press), Mill (distance communication and free speech), Arendt (mass media and totalitarianism), and Berners-Lee (the Web). Knowing who argued what — and when — is essential for applying their ideas to new contexts.

questions.question: Which of the following terms were coined by George Orwell in Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949)? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Doublethink, Newspeak, memory hole, and Big Brother all appear in Nineteen Eighty-Four and have since entered general English usage. 'The marketplace of ideas' was John Stuart Mill's phrase from On Liberty (1859). 'The banality of evil' was Hannah Arendt's concept, coined in her coverage of Adolf Eichmann's 1961 trial in Jerusalem.
